title: Causal pit-wave and field-regime features
summary: F1 pit calls are not independent: strategy groups react to the wider race picture, and pit waves often form around shared tyre windows, safety-car opportunities, or weather changes.
rationale: F1 pit calls are not independent: strategy groups react to the wider race picture, and pit waves often form around shared tyre windows, safety-car opportunities, or weather changes. Current features are mostly driver-local; using only current and past lap field information can expose these collective regimes without future leakage.
implementation_hint: Within each Race_Year and LapNumber, build leave-one-driver-out counts/rates for PitStop on current lap and previous 1-3 laps, by whole field, compound, stint, position band, and wet/slick group. Add indicators such as driver_not_pitted_but_field_pitting, same_compound_pit_wave, previous_lap_pit_wave_decay, and first_large_pit_wave_of_race.
expected_effect: Should improve ranking of borderline pit-window laps, especially when many drivers stop in adjacent laps; likely small but robust AUC lift over fixed tyre-life thresholds.
risk: If Race_Year grouping is reconstructed incorrectly or rows are not sorted causally, these features can leak. Field-wide counts may overfit repeated synthetic patterns if validation is row-random.
